Common .dockerignore entries: .git (large history), node_modules (rebuilt in Docker), __pycache__ (Python cache), .env (local development), logs, test files. A smaller build context means faster builds. Example: a .git directory with 500MB history sent to Docker daemon wastes time. Conversely, .gitignore prevents committing build artifacts (dist/, node_modules/) which shouldn't be in version control. Best practice: use both to exclude unnecessary files from both Git and Docker. Some files (like .env.local) should be in .gitignore but NOT .dockerignore (you might want them in Docker). Think about what each layer does: Git prevents committing, Docker prevents sending to daemon.
example:
# .dockerignore file
.git
.gitignore
node_modules/
__pycache__/
.pytest_cache/
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local
*.log
logs/
dist/
build/
.DS_Store
.vscode/
.idea/
coverage/
.nyc_output/
*.swp
.cache/
README.md
CONTRIBUTING.md
LICENSE

# .gitignore file (different purpose)
node_modules/
__pycache__/
.pytest_cache/
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local
*.log
logs/
dist/
build/
.DS_Store
.vscode/
.idea/
coverage/
.nyc_output/
*.swp
.cache/

# Dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .  # Respects .dockerignore
RUN npm ci

# Only application code is copied, not .git or node_modules from host
# This reduces layer size and build time

question: What is container orchestration and why is Kubernetes the standard?
answer: Container orchestration automates deployment, scaling, networking, and management of containers across multiple hosts. Without orchestration, manually managing hundreds/thousands of containers across a cluster is impractical. Orchestration tools: start containers on appropriate nodes based on resource requirements, restart failed containers, scale applications up/down, manage networking and storage, and perform rolling updates. Kubernetes is the industry standard because: it's open-source (not locked to a cloud provider), supports any container runtime, has extensive ecosystem (Helm, operators, service meshes), provides declarative configuration enabling GitOps, and has strong community support. Alternatives: Docker Swarm is simpler but less powerful, cloud-native options (ECS, App Engine) are proprietary, Nomad is more general-purpose. Kubernetes abstracts the underlying infrastructure—you declare desired state, and Kubernetes reconciles actual state. This enables portability: same Kubernetes manifests run on local, on-prem, or cloud clusters. Learning curve is steep (many concepts, CRDs, controllers), but it's essential for cloud-native development. For teams new to containers, start simple with Docker Compose locally, graduate to Kubernetes for production.
example:
# Without orchestration: manual management
docker run -d --name app1 --cpus 0.5 -m 512m myapp:1.0
docker run -d --name app2 --cpus 0.5 -m 512m myapp:1.0
docker run -d --name app3 --cpus 0.5 -m 512m myapp:1.0
# If app1 crashes, manually restart
docker start app1

# With orchestration (Kubernetes): declarative
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:1.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi

# Kubernetes automatically:
# - Creates 3 pods
# - Schedules them on available nodes
# - Restarts failed pods
# - Rolls out updates
# - Scales up/down based on demand

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas 5

question: How do you manage multiple Kubernetes clusters and cross-cluster communication?
answer: Managing multiple clusters is essential for high availability, disaster recovery, and multi-region deployments. Challenges include consistent configuration across clusters, shared secrets/credentials, and inter-cluster networking. Solutions: GitOps tools (ArgoCD, Flux) deploy the same manifests to multiple clusters, reducing manual configuration. Cluster API automates cluster provisioning and updates. For credentials, use external secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) integrated with Kubernetes. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) enables cross-cluster service communication through automatic mTLS and traffic management. Federation (KubeFed) synchronizes resources across clusters, but is less popular than GitOps approaches. For multi-region: use separate Kubernetes clusters per region/zone, replicate DNS across regions, implement backup/restore between clusters. Disaster recovery: back up etcd regularly, test restore procedures, maintain infrastructure-as-code (Terraform). Multi-cluster observability: centralize logging (ELK, Datadog), metrics (Prometheus), and tracing (Jaeger) across clusters. High availability: distribute replicas across zones, use load balancers, implement circuit breakers. Complexity increases significantly with multiple clusters; ensure team is prepared.
example:
# GitOps for multi-cluster consistency
# Repository structure
k8s-infrastructure/
├── base/
│   └── myapp/
│       ├── deployment.yaml
│       └── service.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── us-east-prod/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   └── cluster-config.yaml
    └── eu-west-prod/
        ├── kustomization.yaml
        └── cluster-config.yaml

# ArgoCD setup for multiple clusters
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: eu-west-cluster
  namespace: argocd
type: Opaque
stringData:
  name: eu-west-prod
  server: https://eu-west-k8s.example.com:6443
  config: |
    {
      "bearerToken": "<token>",
      "tlsClientConfig": {
        "insecure": false,
        "caData": "<cert>"
      }
    }

# Deploy same app to multiple clusters
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: ApplicationSet
metadata:
  name: myapp-multi-cluster
spec:
  generators:
  - clusters:
      selector:
        matchLabels:
          env: production
  template:
    metadata:
      name: 'myapp-{{name}}'
    spec:
      source:
        repoURL: https://github.com/myorg/k8s-infrastructure
        path: overlays/{{name}}
      destination:
        server: '{{server}}'
        namespace: production

# Cross-cluster service discovery with Istio
# Install Istio on multiple clusters with shared root CA
istioctl install --set values.global.multiCluster.clusterName=us-east

# ServiceEntry for remote cluster services
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: ServiceEntry
metadata:
  name: remote-service
spec:
  hosts:
  - remote-service.remote-ns.svc.cluster.local
  ports:
  - number: 80
    name: http
    protocol: HTTP
  location: MESH_EXTERNAL
  endpoints:
  - address: remote-service.remote-ns.svc.cluster.local
    ports:
      http: 80

question: What are CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions) and how do you create them?
answer: Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) extend Kubernetes API to support domain-specific resources. Instead of using generic Pods/Deployments, you define custom resources (e.g., Database, Certificate, Application) matching your domain. A CRD defines the schema (properties, validation), and operators implement the logic to manage instances. CRDs enable declarative infrastructure: declare desired state, and operators handle implementation. Examples: Cert-Manager CRD (Certificate) automates TLS, Istio CRDs (VirtualService, DestinationRule) manage traffic, Prometheus CRD (PrometheusRule) defines alerting rules. Creating a CRD: define the schema (name, properties, validation), create the CRD resource, then operators can watch instances and react. This operator pattern (CRD + controller) is how Kubernetes extensions work. Popular operators: cert-manager, prometheus-operator, sealed-secrets, etc. CRDs are powerful but add complexity—only use when needed. Validation ensures correctness; conversion allows schema evolution. CRDs integrate seamlessly with kubectl: kubectl get mycustomresource works naturally.
example:
# Create a CRD for a custom Database resource
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.mycompany.com
spec:
  group: mycompany.com
  names:
    kind: Database
    plural: databases
    singular: database
    shortNames:
    - db
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true
    storage: true
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            required:
            - engine
            - storage
            properties:
              engine:
                type: string
                enum: ['postgres', 'mysql', 'mongodb']
              storage:
                type: string
                description: Storage size (e.g., 10Gi)
              replicas:
                type: integer
                minimum: 1
                maximum: 5
                default: 3
              backup:
                type: object
                properties:
                  enabled:
                    type: boolean
                  interval:
                    type: string
          status:
            type: object
            properties:
              phase:
                type: string
                enum: ['Creating', 'Ready', 'Updating', 'Failed']
              readyReplicas:
                type: integer

# Use the CRD
apiVersion: mycompany.com/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: myapp-db
spec:
  engine: postgres
  storage: 50Gi
  replicas: 3
  backup:
    enabled: true
    interval: 24h

# Operator controller watches Database resources and creates actual Deployments/Services
# kubectl get databases
# kubectl describe db myapp-db
# kubectl delete db myapp-db

question: What is container sprawl and how do you prevent it?
answer: Container sprawl is uncontrolled proliferation of containers leading to management chaos, security risks, and resource waste. Causes include: lack of governance, no image lifecycle management (old images remain), unused containers, lack of standardization, and inadequate monitoring. Prevention strategies: implement image registry policies (enforce scanning, require approvals), maintain container inventory (track what's running), set lifecycle policies (delete images after X days), use naming standards (descriptive names enabling categorization), enforce resource limits preventing resource exhaustion, and implement governance (approval workflows). Regular audits identify unused containers/images. Tagging strategy (semantic versioning, environment labels) helps tracking. Image scanning detects vulnerabilities, preventing deployment of insecure images. Cost monitoring alerts to unexpected container proliferation. Container orchestration (Kubernetes) helps—it replaces ad-hoc container management. Automation via GitOps prevents manual container creation. Registry quotas limit storage. Define clear ownership (which team owns which containers).
example:
# Prevent container sprawl
# 1. Image policy: require scanning before push
kubectl create namespace image-policy-webhook
# Deploy image policy webhook

# 2. Prevent old image accumulation
reg purge {registry-url} \
  --keep-last 5 \
  --keep-tag-regex '^(main|release-.*)$'

# 3. Resource limits prevent one container consuming all resources
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: prevent-sprawl
spec:
  hard:
    pods: '100'              # Max 100 pods per namespace
    requests.cpu: '50'
    requests.memory: '100Gi'
    limits.cpu: '100'
    limits.memory: '200Gi'

# 4. Container naming standards
# Good: {app}-{env}-{component}
#   myapp-prod-api
#   myapp-staging-worker

# Bad: container1, test, temp, mycontainer

# 5. Inventory and cleanup policy
# Regular cleanup of dangling images
docker image prune -a --force
docker container prune --force

# 6. Tag images with lifecycle
# Image tags: v1.0.0, v1.0.0-prod, v1.0.0-staging
# Cleanup old tags automatically

# 7. Monitor container count
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces | wc -l
kubectl get images | grep 'Created' | awk '{print $3}' | sort | uniq -c

question: How do you implement service discovery in Kubernetes?
answer: Service discovery enables pods to find and communicate with each other dynamically, essential since pod IPs change frequently. Kubernetes provides built-in DNS-based discovery: Services get stable DNS names (servicename.namespace.svc.cluster.local), and pods can reach them by hostname. The kube-dns or CoreDNS service in the cluster handles DNS resolution. Service discovery works at multiple levels: within namespace (servicename), across namespaces (servicename.namespace.svc.cluster.local), and external services (via Ingress). Environment variables are another mechanism: kubelet injects SERVICE_HOST and SERVICE_PORT environment variables for each service, but DNS is preferred. Headless services (clusterIP: None) enable direct pod-to-pod discovery, useful for StatefulSets. External service discovery (from outside the cluster) uses Ingress or LoadBalancer Services. Service mesh adds advanced features: automatic load balancing, circuit breaking, and observability. DNS caching at pod level can cause stale entries; clients should handle DNS resolution frequently. For debugging, use kubectl exec to check DNS: nslookup servicename or kubectl debug.
example:
# ClusterIP Service enables DNS-based discovery
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: myapp
  namespace: default
spec:
  selector:
    app: myapp
  type: ClusterIP
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080

# Pod can access via DNS name
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: client
spec:
  containers:
  - name: client
    image: curlimages/curl
    command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - |
      curl http://myapp
      curl http://myapp.default
      curl http://myapp.default.svc
      curl http://myapp.default.svc.cluster.local

# Headless service (no clusterIP)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mysql
spec:
  clusterIP: None  # Headless
  selector:
    app: mysql
  ports:
  - port: 3306

# StatefulSet using headless service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: mysql
spec:
  serviceName: mysql  # Links to headless service
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: mysql
        image: mysql:8.0

# Access specific pods
# mysql-0.mysql.default.svc.cluster.local
# mysql-1.mysql.default.svc.cluster.local

# Environment variable discovery
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: client
spec:
  containers:
  - name: client
    image: ubuntu
    env:
    # Kubelet injects these
    - name: MYAPP_SERVICE_HOST
      value: '10.0.0.100'
    - name: MYAPP_SERVICE_PORT
      value: '80'
    command: ['curl', '$(MYAPP_SERVICE_HOST):$(MYAPP_SERVICE_PORT)']

question: What are the best practices for Docker image tagging and versioning?
answer: Image tagging strategy is crucial for reproducibility, rollback capability, and clarity. Semantic versioning (v1.0.0, v1.1.0, v2.0.0) clearly communicates API compatibility: major changes increment major version, features increment minor, bug fixes increment patch. Tags should be immutable once pushed to production; never reuse tags like 'latest' for different builds. Always use specific versions in production: avoid 'latest' (unpredictable), instead use explicit versions. For development, 'latest' or 'main' is acceptable, but never in production deployments. Additional tags: environment-based (prod, staging), commit hash (git-abc123 enables traceability), timestamp (2024-03-24-151030). Multiple tags for one image: v1.1.0, v1.1, v1, latest all point to the same image, reducing storage. Image scanning: tag and scan before promoting to production. Registry management: organize images by application (registry/myapp/api:v1.0.0 vs registry/myapp-api:v1.0.0). Document tagging strategy in team wiki. Automate version incrementing in CI/CD. Retention policies: keep last N versions, clean up old images.
example:
# Semantic versioning
v1.0.0        # Major.Minor.Patch
v1.1.0        # Minor bump (feature)
v1.1.1        # Patch bump (bug fix)
v2.0.0        # Major bump (breaking change)

# Build and tag with multiple tags
docker build -t myapp:1.2.0 .
docker tag myapp:1.2.0 myapp:1.2
docker tag myapp:1.2.0 myapp:1
docker tag myapp:1.2.0 myapp:latest

# Development tagging
docker build -t myapp:main -t myapp:main-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) .
docker build -t myapp:$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S) .

# Push all tags
docker push myapp:1.2.0
docker push myapp:1.2
docker push myapp:1
docker push myapp:latest

# Production deployment: always use specific version
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:1.2.0  # Specific version, never 'latest'
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# Registry image organization
registry.example.com/myapp/api:v1.2.0
registry.example.com/myapp/worker:v1.2.0
registry.example.com/myapp/nginx:v1.2.0

# Retention policy (keep last 5 production versions)
docker image ls | grep 'myapp:v' | sort -V | head -n -5 | xargs docker rmi

question: How do you manage environment-specific configurations in Kubernetes?
answer: Managing different configurations for dev/staging/prod is essential. Approaches: ConfigMaps for environment-specific config (database hosts, API endpoints), Secrets for credentials (passwords, API keys), or external configuration management (Consul, etcd). Kustomize overlays provide clean separation: base/ contains common resources, overlays/{dev,staging,prod} contain environment-specific patches. This avoids duplicating manifests. Helm values approach: use different values files (values-dev.yaml, values-prod.yaml) with the same chart. Git branch strategy: maintain separate branches per environment, or use same branch with different directories. Avoid hardcoding values in Dockerfiles; pass via environment variables, ConfigMaps, or Secrets. For secrets, never commit to Git; use external managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) injected at runtime. Tool selection: Kustomize is built-in (no extra dependencies), Helm requires learning templating, GitOps tools (ArgoCD) declaratively manage environments. Best practice: version control all non-secret configurations, use automation to promote changes through environments, and implement approval workflows for production changes.
example:
# Kustomize overlay structure
k8s/
├── base/
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── kustomization.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── dev/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   ├── config.yaml          # Dev-specific ConfigMap
    │   └── replicas_patch.yaml  # 1 replica for dev
    ├── staging/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   └── replicas_patch.yaml  # 2 replicas
    └── prod/
        ├── kustomization.yaml
        ├── config.yaml
        └── replicas_patch.yaml  # 3+ replicas

# base/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
namespace: myapp
resources:
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml

# overlays/dev/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
bases:
- ../../base
namespace: myapp-dev
nameSuffix: -dev
patchesStrategicMerge:
- replicas_patch.yaml
configMapGenerator:
- name: app-config
  files:
  - config.yaml

# overlays/dev/config.yaml
LOG_LEVEL: debug
DATABASE_HOST: postgres-dev.example.com
API_TIMEOUT: 10s

# overlays/prod/config.yaml
LOG_LEVEL: info
DATABASE_HOST: postgres-prod.example.com
API_TIMEOUT: 30s

# Deploy to environments
kubectl apply -k overlays/dev
kubectl apply -k overlays/staging
kubectl apply -k overlays/prod

question: What are the main performance optimization techniques for Kubernetes?
answer: Kubernetes performance involves cluster-level (scheduling, networking) and application-level (pod efficiency) optimizations. Cluster-level: ensure resource requests/limits accurately reflect actual usage (too high wastes resources, too low causes eviction), use pod priority to schedule critical workloads first, implement horizontal pod autoscaling to match demand, and use node affinity to co-locate related pods. Network optimization: use NetworkPolicies to restrict traffic, reducing unnecessary communication; use DNS caching; implement service mesh for connection pooling and reuse. Application-level: optimize container images (minimize layers, remove unnecessary dependencies), implement proper health checks preventing cascading failures, use local caching and reduce external calls. Database queries are often bottlenecks—optimize queries and use connection pooling. Monitoring is essential: track resource usage, latency, error rates, and identify bottlenecks. Use kubelet profiling to identify hot paths. Batch operations to reduce API calls. For storage, use appropriate StorageClass with performance characteristics matching workload. CNI plugins vary in performance—evaluate options. Cluster size affects performance; too small clusters are overloaded, too large have overhead.
example:
# Performance-optimized pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: optimized-app
spec:
  priorityClassName: high-priority  # Schedule first
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:optimized-alpine  # Minimal image
    resources:
      requests:      # Accurate requests enable better scheduling
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
      limits:
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 256Mi
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3
    env:
    - name: GOMAXPROCS
      value: '2'
    - name: JAVA_OPTS
      value: '-Xms128m -Xmx256m'

# PriorityClass
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000
globalDefault: false

# Node affinity for co-locating pods
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-affinity
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: node-type
            operator: In
            values:
            - compute-optimized
    podAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: app
              operator: In
              values:
              - cache
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:latest

# Deployment with HPA for auto-scaling
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: myapp-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: myapp
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70

question: What are some common Kubernetes troubleshooting patterns?
answer: Systematic troubleshooting approach: start with kubectl get pods to check pod status, then kubectl describe to see events. Common statuses: Pending (not scheduled), ContainerCreating (image pulling), CrashLoopBackOff (app crashes), ImagePullBackOff (image not found). ImagePullBackOff: check image name/tag spelling, verify registry credentials, ensure registry is accessible. Pending: check resource availability (kubectl describe nodes), node selectors/affinity, or taints/tolerations. CrashLoopBackOff: view logs (kubectl logs) to understand why app crashed, check health probes causing false failures, or verify dependencies are available. Network issues: check service endpoints (kubectl get endpoints), test DNS (kubectl exec -it -- nslookup), check network policies. Performance issues: use kubectl top to check resource usage, verify requests/limits, enable profiling. Failed updates: check deployment history (kubectl rollout history), view pod events, check resource availability during update. Pod can't communicate: verify service exists and selects the pod (kubectl get svc, kubectl get endpoints), check network policies, verify port mappings. For persistent failures, check logs of previous container (kubectl logs --previous).
example:
# Troubleshooting workflow

# 1. Check pod status
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

# 2. View pod logs
kubectl logs <pod-name>          # Current container
kubectl logs <pod-name> -p        # Previous container
kubectl logs <pod-name> -c <container>  # Specific container
kubectl logs -f <pod-name>       # Follow logs

# 3. Check events
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# 4. Exec into pod for interactive debugging
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/bash
kubectl exec <pod-name> -- curl http://localhost:8080/health

# 5. Check networking
kubectl get svc,endpoints -A
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- nslookup service-name
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- curl service-name:port

# 6. Check resource availability
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl describe nodes
kubectl get resourcequota -A

# 7. Check deployment
kubectl rollout history deployment/<name>
kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>

# 8. Port forward for testing
kubectl port-forward svc/<service-name> 8080:8080

# 9. Debug mode
kubectl debug <pod-name> -it --image=ubuntu

# 10. Check API logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-apiserver-<node>
kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-scheduler-<node>

question: What are the differences between Kubernetes Deployments and StatefulSets?
answer: Deployments manage stateless applications with interchangeable pods—any pod can be replaced with another. Pods have random names, no guaranteed identity, and stateless storage (or shared). Updates roll out in parallel (faster), and replicas scale elastically. Use Deployments for APIs, web servers, microservices. StatefulSets manage stateful applications requiring stable identity: each pod has an ordinal name (myapp-0, myapp-1) guaranteed to stay the same across restarts, persistent storage (each pod gets its own PVC), and ordered lifecycle (pods created/updated sequentially). Pods are created from myapp-0 onwards; termination is reverse order. DNS names are predictable (myapp-0.myapp.default.svc.cluster.local), enabling direct communication. StatefulSets are slower to update (sequential vs parallel) but essential for databases, message queues, and clustered applications. RollingUpdate strategy for StatefulSets updates in reverse ordinal order, allowing controlled rollout. Deployments are simpler (start with these), StatefulSets add complexity but provide guarantees needed for stateful workloads.
example:
# Deployment (stateless)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-server
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:latest

# Pods: web-server-xyz1, web-server-xyz2, ... (random names)
# Replacement: any pod can replace any other
# Storage: shared or temporary
# Update: parallel rollout

# StatefulSet (stateful)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: mysql
spec:
  serviceName: mysql
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mysql
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mysql
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: mysql
        image: mysql:8.0
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/mysql
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ['ReadWriteOnce']
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi

# Pods: mysql-0, mysql-1, mysql-2 (stable names)
# Each pod has persistent storage (mysql-0-data, mysql-1-data)
# Direct access: mysql-0.mysql, mysql-1.mysql
# Update: sequential (mysql-0 first, then mysql-1, mysql-2)

# Key differences table:
# | Feature | Deployment | StatefulSet |
# | Pod Names | Random (xyz1, xyz2) | Ordered (0, 1, 2) |
# | Identity | Interchangeable | Unique |
# | Storage | Shared/Temporary | Per-pod persistent |
# | Update | Parallel (fast) | Sequential (slow) |
# | Use Case | Stateless apps | Databases, brokers |
# | Service | ClusterIP | Headless (clusterIP: None) |

question: What is Kubernetes operator pattern and when should you use it?
answer: The operator pattern combines CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions) with a controller to manage domain-specific applications. An operator encodes operational knowledge: how to deploy, scale, update, and troubleshoot an application. Instead of writing Helm charts or manual manifests, you declare desired state via a CRD, and the operator implements the logic. Example: Prometheus Operator lets you declare PrometheusRule resources, and the operator creates Prometheus instances managing those rules. Operators automate tasks that would otherwise require manual intervention: database schema migrations, backup/restore, scaling based on application-specific metrics, updating dependent services when primary changes. Benefits: automation reduces operational burden, consistency across deployments, complex workflows encoded reliably. Drawbacks: operators add complexity, require debugging multiple controllers, learning curve steep. Operators are appropriate for complex software (databases, message queues, ML platforms) where operational knowledge is substantial. For simple applications, Deployments + ConfigMaps suffice. The operator landscape is growing (OperatorHub.io). Popular operators: prometheus-operator, cert-manager, sealed-secrets. Writing custom operators requires understanding Kubernetes controller patterns and Go programming (though frameworks exist).
example:
# Custom operator example: Database backup operator
# User declares intent via CRD
apiVersion: backup.example.com/v1
kind: DatabaseBackup
metadata:
  name: myapp-backup
spec:
  database: myapp-postgres
  schedule: '0 2 * * *'  # Daily 2 AM
  retentionDays: 30
  destination: s3://backups/myapp

# Operator controller watches DatabaseBackup resources and:
# 1. Creates CronJob for scheduling
# 2. Manages backup scripts
# 3. Uploads to S3
# 4. Cleans up old backups
# 5. Updates status

status:
  phase: Scheduled
  lastBackupTime: 2024-03-23T02:00:00Z
  lastBackupStatus: Success
  nextBackupTime: 2024-03-24T02:00:00Z

# Cert-manager operator example (real-world)
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: tls-cert
spec:
  secretName: tls-secret
  duration: 2160h  # 90 days
  renewBefore: 720h  # 30 days before expiry
  issuerRef:
    name: letsencrypt-prod
    kind: Issuer
  dnsNames:
  - example.com
  - www.example.com

# Cert-manager operator automatically:
# - Requests certificates from Let's Encrypt
# - Manages renewal before expiry
# - Stores in Secret
# - Updates when certificate renews

# Using operator pattern vs manual approach
# Manual: write scripts to backup, schedule cron jobs, manually restore on failure
# Operator: declare intent, operator handles everything, consistent, testable, observable

question: What are Kubernetes events and how do you use them for debugging?
answer: Kubernetes events are records of things happening in the cluster—pod creation, scaling, errors, warnings. Every resource (pod, deployment, node) has an associated list of events describing state changes and issues. Events are time-limited (usually retained for 1 hour) and stored in etcd, viewable via kubectl get events. Events provide crucial debugging information: why a pod won't start, why a node is not ready, why an update failed. Common event types: Normal (informational), Warning (potential issue), Error (failed operation). Resource events: kubectl describe pod shows events for that pod, kubectl describe node shows node-level events. Cluster-wide events: kubectl get events -A shows all events. Events indicate root causes: ImagePullBackOff event means image pull failed (check image name, credentials, registry), Pending event means scheduling issue (insufficient resources), CrashLoopBackOff event shows container keeps crashing (check logs for why). Event fields: time, type (Normal/Warning), reason, message, object (which resource), count (if repeated). Limitations: events are ephemeral (lost after an hour), not queryable like logs, and aggregated (if the same event repeats, count increments rather than new events). For long-term storage, export events to external system (logging, monitoring).
example:
# View cluster events
kubectl get events
kubectl get events -A
kubectl get events -n myapp
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Events for specific resource
kubectl describe pod myapp-abc123
# Shows events like:
# Events:
# Type    Reason     Age       From                  Message
# ----    ------     ----      ----                  -------
# Normal  Scheduled  10s       default-scheduler     Successfully assigned myapp to node-1
# Normal  Pulling    10s       kubelet, node-1       Pulling image "myapp:1.0"
# Normal  Pulled     5s        kubelet, node-1       Successfully pulled image
# Normal  Created    5s        kubelet, node-1       Created container
# Normal  Started    5s        kubelet, node-1       Started container

# Events for deployment
kubectl describe deployment myapp
# Shows events:
# Type    Reason             Age      From                   Message
# ----    ------             ----     ----                   -------
# Normal  ScaledUp           10s      deployment-controller  Scaled up replica set myapp-xyz to 3

# Node events
kubectl describe node node-1
# Shows events:
# Type    Reason                      Age       Message
# ----    ------                      ----      -------
# Normal  NodeHasNoDiskPressure       1h        Node node-1 status is now: NodeHasDiskPressure
# Normal  NodeAllocatableEnforced     1h        Updated Node Allocatable
# Warning InsufficientMemory          30m       Node node-1 has InsufficientMemory

# Export events to external system (Datadog, ELK)
apiVersion: v1
kind: EventExportConfig
metadata:
  name: event-export
spec:
  sink: https://your-log-system.example.com/events
  retentionDays: 30

question: What are the main differences between container runtimes (Docker, containerd, CRI-O)?
answer: Container runtimes execute containers. Docker was the original runtime but has been split into separate components: containerd (runtime) + dockerd (daemon). Kubernetes supports multiple runtimes through the Container Runtime Interface (CRI). Containerd is lightweight, CNCF-graduated, and is the default in many distributions (EKS, GKE). It's faster, uses less memory, and has fewer dependencies than Docker. CRI-O is RedHat-backed, minimal, designed specifically for Kubernetes, and optimized for Kubernetes workflows. It's also CNCF-graduated. Docker is feature-rich but heavier; it's still popular for development but less common in production. Runtime differences: speed (CRI-O and containerd are faster), resource usage (CRI-O < containerd < Docker), features (Docker has most), and Kubernetes integration (CRI-O optimized). Kubelet communicates with runtimes via CRI protocol, abstracting the specific runtime. Cluster can't easily change runtimes once running—it's a deployment decision. Most production clusters use containerd today due to performance and reliability. For development, Docker is convenient; for production, containerd or CRI-O are preferred. Image format (OCI) is standardized, so switching runtimes doesn't require re-building images.
example:
# Check runtime in Kubernetes
kubectl get nodes -o wide
# Shows Container Runtime column

kubectl get node <node-name> -o jsonpath='{.status.nodeInfo.containerRuntimeVersion}'
# Output: docker://20.10.0 or containerd://1.6.0 or cri-o://1.27.0

# Runtime configuration in kubelet
# /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
# or /etc/sysconfig/kubelet
CUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS="--container-runtime=containerd --container-runtime-endpoint=unix:///var/run/containerd/containerd.sock"

# Docker runtime
CUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS="--container-runtime=docker"

# containerd socket
CUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS="--container-runtime=remote --container-runtime-endpoint=unix:///var/run/containerd/containerd.sock"

# CRI-O socket
CUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS="--container-runtime=remote --container-runtime-endpoint=unix:///var/run/crio/crio.sock"

# Performance comparison (approximate)
# Docker: 
#   - Image size: ~20MB (full daemon)
#   - Memory: 100-200MB overhead
#   - Pull speed: moderate
#   - Features: extensive (build, registry, compose)

# containerd:
#   - Image size: ~5MB
#   - Memory: 20-50MB overhead  
#   - Pull speed: fast
#   - Features: minimal (runtime only)

# CRI-O:
#   - Image size: ~3MB
#   - Memory: 10-30MB overhead
#   - Pull speed: fastest
#   - Features: K8s-specific only

# Runtime interaction (same OCI images)
# Image: nginx:latest (OCI format)
# Both runtimes execute it identically
# No rebuild needed when switching runtimes
